I enjoyed the structure of the lab with the coding challenges. The short introduction lectures had some good advice/tips but sometimes felt a little phoned in. Attending the labs really help getting questions answered. The exams were relatively easy if you did the labs - you really need to stay on top of them. Overall a decent class and Prof.

Puts trick questions on tests. This is especially dumb b/c it's a programming class done and checked without a computer. For example she would put a capital O instead of a 0 to trick us. What's the point? Hard to understand, homework and assignment are unclear. Stay far away.

Avoid Anne like the plague. She will ruin your semester if you don't already have a good background in C. She leaves lectures early and is demeaning when you ask her questions in her office hours and will not make time for you outside those hours. I can't believe SFU has allowed her to teach for as long as she had.

lectures are useful SOMETIMES when she guides us through writing code step by step , but she doesn't do that often later on in the course. Didn't do any of the required readings but her weekly exercises and assignments are VERY HELPFUL to help you understand python. If you are a cmpt major can't pass 120, change majors, don't blame the prof.

Anne Lavergne was awesome! She could be a tough grader, there were lots of assignments, however she was always available to help out, and was very reasonable to deal with. She gave us plenty of extra time to complete assignments when the grading robot was down, and as long as you stay on top of the assignments you will be ok. Do NOT fall behind.

Anne is a nice person but her lectures are all over the place. I've had no previous knowledge in coding and this really hit me like a train. Assignments are extremely time consuming and not worth a whole lot. The lectures are useless. The exams are super hard for an "introduction course". You have to spend a lot of time learning on your own.

She is very knowledgeable but doesn't even know how to explain and the exams are kinda hard. if you are thinking about taking this course with her as an elective and aiming for a A+ let me tell you its really hard not possible for me. She doesn't even explain properly and lectures waste of time since no information is given. Just Relax and Enjoy!

Her lectures are pretty much useless. For an 'introductory' course, the assignments she gives are too advanced. The requirements she sets for each assignment are utterly ridiculous and time-consuming, yet they're hardly worth anything. Exams have lots of trick questions and you only get 45 minutes to do them. Average for the first midterm was 48%.
Worst professor ever.

Anne is very nice and enthusiastic but the way she teaches an introduction computer science course is as if you already have a basic knowledge of the program. No need to attend lectures since the course is basically just readings. Would be better if she broke it down and explained what things meant.

Professor Lavergne was awesome! She really cares about her students and is very approachable in office hours. She wants her students to succeed, but does make you work for it. She gave us additional time for the lab assignments when the grading robot was down, in some cases, much after the original deadline, which was very kind of her.

CMPT 127 is taught the same way year over year with the exactly same materials. It's doable, but you need to cross your fingers to not get profs like Anne Lavergne who makes the course so much worse. Not caring about students and incompetent TAs aside, she asked students to type the entire file in final exam casually takes 40% off for a typo.

Literally the worst professor you'll ever experience. You might as well bend over and take it all day and night if you're going to enroll in her class. There's a reason the department had to come into her class on several occasions and see how she was teaching because she had received so many complaints via email due to her teaching style. It's bad
Nice prof but gives 10 minutes for a quiz which would take 15-20 minutes to do. Exams are hard. Study, study, study.
she does not care about the things you write in the exam, all she cares is about the neatness and the drawing. if your exam is colourful, you get full marks. her lectures are useless, dont take her.
Avoid her if you want to learn something, clearly she should learn how to teach.... what a terrible lecturer

Your exams will be 25% or more on material you have spent very little time (or no time at all) in lectures or in assignments. This will not change whether it is her first time teaching a course or her last. Our final had one question worth 26% on an important topic she spent essentially 1 lecture on. Very knowledgeable about the material though.

ANNE requiresstudents to do lots of assignments in this course, i dont know if just because this was the first time for her to teach this course. lecture were messy, You need to spend lots of time doing your assignmetns. Final are so hard, especially foucsing on the last topic of lecture which was not quite explained during the last lecture

Man such a heavy emphasis on the final exam on the last lecture! Study guide useless; basically read everything. Notes are often messily done as a result of not finishing the lecture on time, makes me late for next lecture or else I miss notes which can't be found anywhere else. Office hours are helpful though +0.01.
Her exam question is made from nowhere. cmpt 295 is already a tough course. She gives lots of homework and assignment. Everything is hard about her.

While 295 is a course that's naturally heavy (and redundant in my opinion), Anne teaches it WAY better that Brad Bart (he's the worst). She can really be an asset if you go to the office hours, sometimes they're empty, other times there's a long lineup. Bottom line, taking 295 with her is basically taking the lesser of 2 evils.
never lets us out on time

she's the worst prof ever, makes a computer course like a memorize course. You will learn nothing of improving your programming. And useless for your coop. If you want to learn something, don't choose her. And she also can't explain the lectures well, even for the assignment requirements, she suddenly changed the rules after we finished it.

Really tough class. Assignments are difficult and unclear - make friends in this class and work together. the assignments are only worth 3-5% for 5 of them but take up up to 1-2 days to do each one but there is lots of coding so you learn a lot. If you don't do the assignments you will do poorly on exams. Anne's office hours are very helpful

This class was by no means easy. it is meant to be a gpa booster;however, if you take this class with her you will probably regret it too. the average was a C which is the lowest that I have ever heard of in my 4 years at SFU. the messages left on here are misleading of how hard the class actually is. save yourself by choosing another class.

She shows to have a nice personality, but sometimes it fools you to think the class is gonna be chill. Oh hell no. This class is no joke, if you leave materials to the last night, then rip. She will make you work your @$$ off to get things done. Confusing assignments and labs, and sometimes bugs in the code. Not the best prof in CMPT department.

Pros: She has a nice personality. She sometimes posts pictures and solutions to problems. She is willing to help. Cons: - Won't learn much C++ during lectures. You must have 50% in the final to pass the course, and also 50% must be the average of your final and midterm together. Average Prof

Really good professor. Seems to care about students. Asks for feedback on how to make the course work for students, and tries to implement it. Exams are of average difficulty. The assignments are fun, and often allow for some creativity.

As a lecturer she's not bad, entertaining even, and her tests are fine; but her code is some of the most awful stuff I have ever laid my eyes upon. Take CMPT 225 with Anne Lavergne just to learn how NOT to write C++.
Needs to prepare students for assignments and tests rather than go over the same unclear, bad examples over and over

I had her for both CMPT127 and 225. She wasn't much help in 127, which is probably how the course was supposed to be in the first place. But she was terrific in 225. Teaches very well, encourages questions (and answers them well), is always available for help and is very engaging. Word loads are reasonable, and assignments are well thought out.
Easy exam

Great professor that doesn't spoon feed you information, which helps if you have a true interest in programming. Assignments were fair and quite easy if you put in the work, same goes for the exams - sometimes there are even bonus marks available!. She's a kind and bubbly person as well. Very happy to have taken my first CMPT class with her.

Terrible lectures, even worse tests. Very disorganized. Programming projects are very poorly thought out. Asks for good programming style, but has terrible programming habits herself. Likes to ask "trick" questions. Won't post class notes because otherwise no one would attend her lectures. Yes, it's that bad...

she goes at a good pace and explains the concepts very well even though her code it'self isnt well written or structured. Finding your preferred coding structure is your own job. She is the right kind of prof for those who will be experimenting with coding on their own but she will be terrible if you only intend to code for the sake of assignments.

Gives you practically no time for exams and quizzes. Often uses methods in her program that she's never discussed during lecture in quiz questions. Sample programs are littered with syntax errors. Terrible analogies that often confuse me more than clear things up. Whatever you do, find a different prof.

She makes up terms that are not used in industry or theory and expects us to memorize them. Writes absolutely awful code with terrible inconsistencies in commenting, naming conventions, whitespace etc. Makes it hard to follow and doesnt explain stuff well. Posts her notes sheets to fill in litterally 30 mins before class. Exams have mistakes. BAD

Whatever you do, FIND A DIFFERENT PROF. Spends too much time trying to re-organize papers and talks about simple concepts that barely relate to assignments or exams. Only gives 12 minutes for tests/quizzes and 40 minutes for the midterm. She's really nice and will try to help during office hours, but that's all I can say. I gave up in the end.

Does not know how to teach or make lecture slides. Spends too much time on easy concepts and not enough on the hard ones. Will spend 30mins of a 1hr lecture to do i-clicker and announcements, leaving no time for actual lecturing. And worst of all, literally spent 1hr playing with paper for a lecture. PLAYING WITH DUCKING PAPER!

Worst 'professor' ever. Doesn't explain hard concepts, wastes a bunch of time explaining easy concepts with hand waving, terrible analogies, and a bunch of scribbling. Assigns an entire assignment and practice problems at lecture 2 days before the midterm. If you have to take it with Anne, avoid class, read the text, and code.

not sure y everyone hates her. she's really nice and explains very clearly. she knows very well what she's talking about. very helpful if u need help. she set up amazing extra tutorial sections to prepare for final, and those tutorial sections saved my GPA!

Anne took her time to create long review sessions prior to the midterm and final (and was also available during office hours on top of that). Very nice and clearly likes to offer help. Tip: Do the labs and you will do great.

Her lectures are pretty boring and unorganized, so it's easy to blame her teaching for doing poorly in the class. But, a big part of CS is practicing, so DO THE LABS. Anne is a nice lady, she'll help you if you ask. Don't get too discouraged after the midterm, the average for it was 46% but she made the final easier because of it.

As a first year student i though Anne was amazing. Her quizes, tests, assignments were quite tough but her lectures were immaculate. They were precise, clear, and detailed, oh were they extremely detailed! One important thing to NOTE: YOU MUST ATTEND THE LECTURES. If you attended all the lectures this class is an easy A/B. YOU WILL FAIL IF YOU DONT

The dynamic of the class is a bit flawed. She lectures like the class are rookies, but hands out assignments/quizzes that are geared towards semi-experienced programmers. With that being said, if you have any questions I'm sure she would be happy to assist you. She's a kind and helpful person.

The test are hand written when the entire course is online python use. Half the course is teaching you how to write code and the second half is how to find/fix bugs or what has gone wrong in the code. But cannot use any of that knowledge when on a test because, once again, it's hand written. Lectures are very boring!!
projects are hard,even though you get good marks on it,exams,what can i say about it,cheat sheets are useless?and you won't even know why your marks are so low

Know that Anne's notes and powerpoints are too basic to prepare for her assignments and tests. I highly recommend doing all her labs and practicing coding on your own time. The first month or 2 is important because it lays down the groundwork for programming so don't slack off. Textbook, Codecademy , and online resources are very helpful

Anne Lavergne is a very kind lady who will indeed help you. However, computer science 120 was one of the most difficult classes i've ever taken in my entire life because of her. Her assignments are extremely difficult, her lecture notes are far too simple and vague to use for any of the homeworks, and her midterm is hard. Please avoid this teacher.
Really bad at teaching.. Learnt nothing in her class.... All self study. exams are hard.
Very bad professor. Skims through the hard content. How is this even an "introductory" course?
Avoid at all costs. She focuses on the simple things and makes them confusing, then skims over the difficult stuff. Sparkling mind and unclear

Will work with you to run through your code and see what the issue is. Clear grading scheme, each coding problem is worth around 3%, get 80+% to pass the course. Unlimited tries as the servers will run through each piece of code every 8 hours and will post if code meets requirements or fails.
she give tough assignments and quizzers as well as midterm and final youd better choose another professor..........
Assignments and projects are hard. Her lecture slides are unclear and confusing. Most of time you have to self-study and read textbook.
Do not take cmpt120 with this prof. Her notes are not clear, and all over the place.
Good Luck. I have nothing to say.

Worst cs prof I ever had. She literally reads text book out word by word. This course is highly subjective, I asked her why I got 0 on one of my midterm question but someone else got full mark with essentially the same answer as mine. She answered, "there was nothing wrong with your answer, I just don't like your answer." argh...

(Summer 2013) Since Anne is teaching 127 this summer I thought I'd share my experience. She's genial in class and will help you if you ask, but doesn't spoon-feed or take any BS. Self-study outside of class is a must. Very strict marker. I think she'd be a better 127 instructor than Vaughan, who has an attitude.

Reflecting on it after finishing all my lower level courses she is actually a good prof. She is not like a typical sfu prof for lower division science courses where they spoon feed you by making you fill in the blanks of their notes. Sure Lavergne doesn't have good notes but this is where PRACTICE coding and reading the book actually pays off.

Worst prof ever, garbage notes, wastes half of each class talking about the previous class, ask her a question and she talks around it instead of answering it, has a huge attitude problem, class isn't about programming, course is about reading and memorizing her notes, which are terrible

Lectures are mildly useful, but focus is on simple concepts and are not useful for exams. Exams are poor: There are vague questions, where if your answer differs from the one on the answer sheet, it will be marked wrong even if it is technically correct. Has a poor attitude in office hours and labs. Textbook was mediocre, I used other resources.
She has an attitude when you go to her office hours.

Anne is friendly with OK slides. Mostly learned material through YouTube & another textbook b/c didn't like the assigned one. Asks really vague questions on exams and expects specific answers, but if you talk to her & explain your answer she'll boost your mark generously. Do assignments & labs to understand, but they're not related to exams!

Normally I don't post a comment on this website, but Anne is just ridiculously bad. She is by far the worst CMPT prof I have had in SFU. Useless lecture, retarded assignment, and she has an attitude problem when you go to her office hours. And FYI, she is not a prof cause she doesn't even have a PHD.

if you take java with her, you'd better know everything about java before you take the course. she doesn't teach the key point at all. you have to learn lot of things on your own... huge work on assignment. her lectures are really useless... only 1/4 students show up... because her explanations are really bad..
really boring prof. lectures are useless. don't have the key point. not related to the exams.

Super simple. Honestly, just follow along, ask questions if need be because she's really helpful. As long as you're a logical person, it's straight forward simplistic coding with HTML + CSS for beginners, and two days of Python. Cheat sheet for the midterm and final, 5 easy assignments. Really don't know how someone could do poorly in this class.
She is super nice lady. And her notes are really clear. Kind of boring of the lecture but it s not bad at all.
She is a very boring teacher.

Anne is clueless as to how things are done in the work place today. She has an attitude problem if you contest the classroom material being taught. The other students don't know any better, but for people who actually work in the field the stuff she is preaching is at least 15 years out of date. Avoid her if you would like to learn relevant work.

Not bad. Too theoretical, you won't be coding alot in this class. She has a nice attitude and genuinely wants to help. For assignments, ask for clarification (might get docked marks for silly things).

Over half the stuff in my binder is her. She gives "partial lecture" notes which forces you to go to lectures, yet when you go to lecture, she forces you to listen to her demo, instead of getting to the point. Her demo's are so boring that you fall asleep and when she does mention the notes required to be written, you are already asleep. 0/100000

Very disorganized prof. Lecture notes are broken down into incoherent parts. Midterm and finals' averages are 50-52% in a non-curved course. About one-third of the class would fail if she did not decrease the maximum marks by 2 or 3. I have never attended a class that is not curved and class average is around 50% before.

The lecture notes are disorganized and unhelpful. (there are more than 10 sets of notes for one Unit). The speed of the professor is unnecessarily slow. Thanks to this class, I lost my interest in Computing Science :(

She was by far not only the worst computer teacher that I had but also the worst instructor that I EVER had. She does not explain anything AT ALL and when you ask her questions she refuses to answer and asks you too google it. Never take any courses with her (I won't anymore) and if you did it is absolutely useseless to go to lectures.

I enjoyed the class, the prof. really tried to make it interesting. The midterms and the final is easy as long as you read the lecture notes. But TAs marked assignments fairly strict, so make sure you have fixed all the details before submitting them.

Unclear notes, tries to be fun in class but extremely unhelpful in person. Refuses to answer "easy" questions because she thinks we should be able to figure it out. Don't take this if you are not a computer science major.
Spends too much time of simple things such a definitions. Doesn't teach any code. Assignments were long and tedious. Doesn't give solutions to assignments. Midterm wasn't that bad. Final was brutal!!
Summary for CMPT 225 Assignment -> lots to do, you must know everything about JAVA before you go lab because she doesn't teach how to write program Lecture -> simply, not effective
She has no idea to teach! She didn't take responsibility to mark your assignment even though you do your assignment all night long,if it can't run,you has won't get any scores!

She is a terrible and useless prof. I learn nothing from her lecture. She only reads her PPT on the class, and never show how the programs run. And she taught so much that almost covered the whole textbook.
She doesn't know how to teach. She doesn't teach clearly.

Memorize everything that she has on her power points, assignments and examples.No need to read Python book, maybe just look over the examples..Try to memorize different codes for creating a web page, because on midterm and the final,the part about web pages was the most important. Also, you can bring your cheat-sheet on any exams.

Shows up late to class, doesn't program in a PROGRAMMING class, teaches ...correction *tries to teach us* stuff that we need to complete our labs a day before the labs are due...midterm average was under 50...and she blames the class? marks reflection of her teaching , by far the worst prof I have had in my 3 years in university.avoid at all costs!
Anders really knows functional programming, types and formal stuff. He is also a really ardent and easy-going guy to students who actually put effort. His seminar course is easy and chill.

Seems like a nice guy; lectures are pretty good and encourages questions/class participation. I do feel like a learned something from this class but the assignment descriptions are pretty confusing and you are punished on exams for trying (negative marks & no part marks). I got 0/20 on 2 questions because I was close but not 100% correct.

Assignments are intense, spend lots of time on them if you want to actually gain anything from them. Lots of readings and they're absolutely necessary, lectures are supplemental. If you need help you need to ask. Rust portion much easier for those used to imperative languages. Overall not easy but not atypical for a CMPT course at this level.

Assignments are easy and typically have 100% averages. Exams are hard but not for the reasons you think. Easy questions but rarely gives part marks (either 0 or full points), so know your stuff. Generous scaling since everyone did bad. Don't listen to the people who failed, you only needed a 35% average on both exams to pass and it's a low bar.

He want us to be compilers rather than understanding the code and write the logic behind it. If there is even a mistake of brackets, he takes away all the marks and give 0. If you are super into details, only then take this course or else you will fail.
Professor Anders Miltner is flat-out terrible. He can't teach, he grades like he enjoys watching students fail, and taking his class is the fastest way to tank your GPA. Avoid him. Seriously.

The assignments are very tough but you learn a lot working through them and his practice midterms are similar to the actual midterms, so I thought the class was fair. His lectures are largely coding examples working through the chapters covered in the readings and he's very enthusiastic and open to any and all questions asked during lectures.

Wack ahh prof. His lectures are so bad and the slides are so vague. With the way he teaches, it sounds like he's trying to learn with the students. Midterms average are pretty low and the coding questions tend to be tricky. Don't take this professor

Anders is a very knowledgeable guy. His lectures he demonstrates the ideas we are talking about. There are small weekly assignments and then larger assignments. The small assignments are not bad, the larger ones are ok for the most part but Anders' class suffers from a lack of clear instructions, which can make the assignments frustrating. Exams ok
Anders is one of the worst profs at SFU. He wants his students to fail, and has created his grading scale to ensure they do. Do not take any courses with him, even if he's your only option.

Don't use AI to get 100% on every assignment and then complain about his "teaching style" after doing poorly on the midterm. He is a great prof, very understanding and helpful when you have questions.
Really hard marker...hard to understand. Instructions for assignments are unclear.

CMPT 384 - One of the worst professors at SFU. Homework is so hard and does not give partial marks in Exams. He does not know how to teach. Expects you to know everything. More than half the class dropped. Only 50 students were left at end.

People keep saying that homework/assignments were just "practice" but fail to realize that if everyone does similarly on the homework/assignments, then the only thing differentiating them is their exam performance. The exams weren't even that unfair for a 3rd year course, (reverse a linked list/check code will compile).
Nice prof, but the course is graded very harshly.

I want to say I liked Anders. He was funny and nice and I liked his assignments, but the final grade distribution was so egregious that it ruined the otherwise not so bad class/teaching style and I can't give him more than a 2.

Anders is a cheerful and competent lecturer. He is approachable after class, willing to answer questions, and has strong interest and knowledge in his field. Attending class is very helpful, if you miss any lectures, reading the provided material is a good alternative. The exam was quite challenging, but partial marks were given and curved.

I took CMPT 383 the first time it was taught by Anders and it was lovely; he has new photos of his pet at the beginning of every lecture, and the assignments are fun, despite that the content gets tricky in the first half. If you email him you'll get back thoughtful responses. If you enjoy programming, you'll love this course and this prof.

Grade is mostly based on the exams even though 60% is on assignments. Very strict grading on exams w/ no partial grades. For example, either you get 0, 5, or 10 out of 10 for a question. You will get penalized for incorrect answers, so make sure to BOX the CORRECT answers. C- to B+ was spread across 70-77% w/ 150 students in the class. C+ average.

First midterm had 3 mistakes and he gave us no extra time, we wasted time reading wrong questions. Teaches horribly, you have to go through the attached readings yourself to learn. Following the slides is very confusing, this guy has never put any ounce of information together without making mistakes in them. Yet if YOU make a mistake, then C-

This was a very stressful course to take. The exams were insanely difficult for both midterms (had 3 typos too). He expects you to be as good as him in coding Haskell and Rust when you've only had 1 and a half months learning each language. He himself is extremely disorganized and has said "I'm too lazy" literally in every class about 5 times.

Fall 2024, he announced this class is no longer GPA booster. The end of the semester, the average is C+, he delivered his promise. Vitamins and Assignments are technically just for practice, he gave out letter grade based on your exam only. His new grade scaling seems ridiculous: B-: 73.99-72.5, C+: 72.49-72, C: 71.99-70.1, C-: 70.09-64.

Anders can be disorganized in his teachings. The lecture slides are riddled with typos and his explanations are hard to follow due to the his pacing. The midterms are tricky and difficult which is worsened by his deceiving methods of giving partial credit. Overall, Anders is an enthusiastic person who falls short as a student-centered lecturer.

Weekly homework and assignments were easy but solely based on conceptual problems. The 2 midterms were ATROCIOUS and lowered everyone's grades (the avg was around a 40% for both). On top of that, he had custom grade cutoffs in which a 70% was a C. It seemed like he was hell-bent on breaking the notion of this class being a GPA booster in the past.

Not bad at teaching, pretty enthusiastic guy. But the exams were very difficult, part marks were hard to earn, the averages were in the 40s. Don't believe his initial grading scheme as most of the class got scaled down 1 to 2 letter grades.

Anders is probably my favorite CMPT professor at SFU. He goes above and beyond to ensure that students understand material, and his lectures are clear and easy to follow. He makes complicated topics seem easy. And he answers emails incredibly quickly. And he's very generous with regrading exams if you have any grading objections. 100% recommend

Agree with the passionate part. Assignments / Weekly programming exercises are fair (some might say a breeze?) Would have appreciated more challenging ones. Doesn't go too deeply into the languages and their features. Course materials heavily rely on the official docs, and are by no means in depth. Exams are A-OK.
Great prof! Passionate about his work and you will learn a lot

Anders was an extremely enthusiastic professor and that showed in his teaching. He was able to use this enthusiasm to teach us quite well. He has clear grading criteria and reasonable weekly small assignments, as well a few big ones. Midterm was a bit difficult, but no final. Just two midterms.

Lecture notes self-contained, concepts clearly explained. Textbook reading is helpful but not required. Prof. Chindelevitch is approachable and helpful answering any questions. If one has a strong math background, this course is not that difficult. Highly recommend - gives a foundation towards computational complexity. Fair exams, HW can be long.
Prepare to learn the topics by reading the textbook and watching other online lectures.
Leonid is passionate about his field and research. He is also extremely knowledgeable. However, teaching doesn't seem to be his main focus.

He is a good prof but not for a CMPT course. He teaches the course like it is a social science course(He wastes too much time on slides than necessary but slides have all the knowledge you need to pass.) so attendance is not needed. Assignments took most of the grade(%60) so it is less stressful. You need to know pre-reqs because he uses them.

Assignments and exams were pretty fair and related to course material. Focused more on theory and pseudocode than actual coding which is good imo since a data structures & algorithms class should try to be language agnostic. Only major cons were posting assignments/study guides late and slow marking on assignments. Good for 1st time teaching 225.

He has a different approach to teaching computing. His assignments have a coding part and written part. Midterm and final had all written questions,mostly about coming up with algorithms, sometimes pseudocode. I liked the idea of testing us heavily on algorithms because you learn a lot of coding in other classes anyway. Slides were self-explanatory

This class in a nutshell: posts study guide Wednesday, posts practice exam Thursday, FINAL EXAM FRIDAY. He literally reads of the slides and if you're kind of new to coding have fun because he does not show any examples of how to code things. He'll give you the algorithms for some functions but you're going to have to learn the rest yourself.

Unorganized, read only from slides, didn't do any examples with class, didn't teach code other than 1st few days. Almost always away when needing to meet. Homework vague marked slowly with little feedback. Forgot to give, needed code for assignment few hours before, deadline. Gave 2 weeks for each assignment for 1st half, 2nd half was given 1 week.

He just reads the lecture slides and never does any kind of coding practice in class. His lecture slides also looks like the complex introduction of the topics that seems that he's reviewing the stuff in his 700 level cmpt classes. The assignments are totally vague. If you actually want to learn then don't take his class.

Seems extremely unorganized. Lectures often make simple concepts seem confusing. Assignments, lectures, and midterms could be mistaken for 3 different courses. Does not really teach material, instead chooses to solve problems in his head and describe his thoughts (basically trying to teach math strictly verbally)

Always busy to actually do said office hours. Unorganized with giving assignments, teaching you to finish said assignments. Funny, nice guy. Wait till a few hours before assignment was due to tell us that his code he gave us to do the assignment was faulty/incomplete. Didn't actually teach how to apply code to problems. was 1st time teaching class
You REALLY need to pay attention during the all the lectures !!! The stuff is difficult and challenging, but you DEFINITELY can learn a lot.
Certainly you can learn a lot from this course. Beware of the difficulty.
Best SFU computer science professor for sure. Recommend it so much.

Leonid is a great yet tough professor. Expect to commit a lot of time in his course. 10% of your grade is participation, so showing up, going to office hours and asking questions gets you easy marks. He is very knowledgeable and easy to talk to. I've never seen anyone recite a lecture from memory before, but he makes it look easy. Would recommend!

Cristina related her content to the tech workplace which was nice, but we had required attendance for 8:30am class, assignments were paced weirdly, and sometimes her criteria was vague or not included in the assignment description but scattered around lecture slides. No exams or big group projects though. She did try to make lectures engaging.

Amazing effort into lectures, I feel bad for Cristina, she tried to make the class as engaging as possible, but 105 is just a dull class, anyway, she's also very generous and forgiving. don't focus on the material you write, just focus on following the rubric, thats where the bulk of the marks are

She's a good prof but the CMPT 105W course sucks. I skipped like 5 weeks worth of lectures and missed nothing. The written reports can be a little tricky, make sure you follow guidelines properly and do your citations properly.

Loved the grading scheme as it was very forgiving. She was a nice prof, but I unfortunately did not find much value from the lectures. Prof and TAs were responsive for questions regarding the assignments. Rubric and what to be expected are clearly laid out, was fairly graded as well. Definitely recommend her for a course like 105w.

Good class if you're ok at writing in general. Assignment marks just have random marks taken off by TAs. Lectures can be boring mainly due to course material but Christina is a good prof. Only con is assignments are due before past assignments are marked so sometimes you can make the same mistake in both assignments
Great prof, it's just that the course itself isn't that fun but besides that if u can yap in English u r good to go especially since TAs r more lenient than highschool teachers!

Super nice and great prof, it's just the content of the course which makes it very boring. I only attended like 2-3 lectures because of this. As long as you participate with the discussions on canvas and do well on essays, basically a free A.
Very good professor

Alright prof, found the course to be boring but most bc of the material and not how it was taught. Grading can depend on if it's a TA or not but the prof herself is very generous. She moved around deadlines sometimes which was nice
Extremely easy course. Don't bother showing up to lectures, they're useless.

Would recommend to avoid if you can. Lectures are incredibly boring. She just reads words from the slides, and says nothing important. You will be asked to finish the next part of the assignment before you are given feedback on the last part, and will be penalized for the same mistakes. If you must take her class, lectures are a waste of time.

Class was very boring in general and professor did not have a lot of energy either which made the lectures worse. Assignments are graded weirdly. Lectures felt very useless as well since she doesn't teach and just goes through slides on her powerpoint without really saying anything important.
She's Awesome
Good prof very understanding changed lots of deadlines she is pretty good not going to lie

This course combined actually a lot of learning without a huge onerous work load. We demoed are projects to the TA which gave immediate feedback. Dr. Funt was struggled having to teach some reasonably dense mathematics to class with a pretty diverse mathematical background but seemed to be able to convey the essential ideas successfully.

Wow, best professor ever met. His lecture is very interesting. The material is challenging in a good way. Assignments are very helpful. One classmate got a well paid job by talking about one of the assignments we did in this course. Prof Funt is very patient to students and their questions. Very supportive for extra helps.

Brian is a great prof for CMPT 102: he has good knowledge in Matlab and he is passionate in teaching it . I would say attending lectures is quite important for this course, as there are "to-tries' for every lecture. It's a great opportunity since you code with ppl there to answer questions. It's an easy A if you do the practice in class.

He looks very kind, but he is quite mean. I got 69% of total and just get C+. The average of class is C+. Exams are very surprising since we haven't done any related questions. He always say "you should be fine", but your marks will be very low if you believe him. The assignment is interesting though.
Great professor. Brian explains the points very clear and the homework was very interesting, although time consuming.

Nice guy with a very hands-on teaching style. My main complaint is that he offers no practice questions even though most of the course is heavy on math, so the only way to study for exams is to understand the abstract concepts and then hope you can solve calculation problems during the exam. If he gave written problems he would be awesome.
Great Professor ! Very kind and helpful.

An approachable educator who definitely knows what he's teaching. His lecture slides are useful, though basic. So, you need to master the in class examples to complete the homework. Though he is decent at explaining things, I found the TA even more helpful than him. My only suggestion for Dr. Funt is that he boil down to simpler terms when teaching

No textbook so you need to go to class. Always available for help and you can always ask questions in class. Allows cheat sheet for exams. Only need short to medium length answers for exams, but you have to think. Course is easier if you have MATH 251, 314 but still okay without it.
Horrible prof, by far one of the worst people i have ever encountered. I hope he does not continue as a professor, at SFU.
Always willining to help. Sometimes confuses himself during lectures. Overall a relatively nice guy
difficult but fair exam questions, easy marking, a very interesting prof, sometimes unclear.
nice prof, very helpful. It's better to learn from him than other computer vision books.Assignments are useful and pratical, exams are relatively hard.
great prof. hard exam.

he doesnt know what he is talking about and his hair is negatively affecting my ability to concentrate in his class this man should learn how to teach and if he continues to persue computer science he should learn how to use a computer

Dr Funt is a great prof, he spearheaded getting this course switched to Matlab which is way more useful for most people who have to take it. He clearly wants the best for his students, and is very helpful. Assignments can be a little tricky, but they're interesting and instructive. All the tests were very fair.
funt is a great prof. Just make sure you dont play on the computers during lecture! he does not like that. just listen and you'll be fine.
I love professor Funt. This class would be a lot harder if it wasn't taught in CSIL. Matlab was a way better language to learn than Java or C++. I thought I would hate it, but this class is awesome.

Dr. Funt is a very helpful,funny, caring teacher. He even me offered extra help outside the course content. One of the best professors I've had. A+ hands down. Very clear and reasonable in all testing and teaching aspects.
Not interesting/helpful/useful...that pretty much sums it up...maybe he's better at teaching courses in his "area of expertise" but he's a crummy first year course prof

One of the best profs out there. He tries his best to help you learn what the course is about. There was some difficult math in this course but he worked hard at making sure you understood it. If you need help he has no problem helping you and is very encouraging.
He's definitely not a nice guy. The worst professor in computer science department.
His exams were easy but irrelated to the course. Don't need any math skill to pass his course. Do not expect to learn anything from him. He is not a good prof.

A very nice prof. Does a pretty good job at making complex concepts easier to understand. Tries to do his best to make you do well. Exams are easy if you studied. Rather interesting assignments. NOTE: This course is not recommended for people with weak math basis.
Great prof, he explains every concept in detail and uses analogies to help with understanding. Puts very good effort into extra help for students that have questions.

This professor is cool! even if the content of this class was extremely easy, its assignments are somewhat fun. If you have time for them (one week of work each) you can take really high marks. He doesn't give lots of informations for the final otherwise it should be too easy to solve.. Weird questions solvable thinking for 2 minutes.

Complex concepts, but the difficulty of tests was balanced. You need to understand all those concepts (hard) but once you did, you'll get reasonably good mark on tests (easy) 'cause the questions themsselves are simple (frankly I expected more chalenges and studied accordingly so maybe I feld it was easy...)

Astonishing professor. He can explain the most complicated things so well and makes the subject so sweet and useful. Although he is very mathematical in class, his tests do not require much math. He just wants to to "understand" the concept. Gives suprisingly high marks! 100% recommended!
woooo mamma
Messy notes. Strict.
Final was an unhappy surprise. Assignments are ambiguous. Not a fun class...
Could have used some practice questions
Hard but awseome prof! Gives VERY high marks.
EXTREMELY HARD!!! But gives us very suprising marks!
B prepare 2 fail the course if u're not confident in ur Math abilities. Period.
No practice questions at all, so expect to see weird crazy questions in final
a very nice guy. very disorganized. forgetful (even those important equations)

I would not recommend. Lectures seem unconnected to what he tests. Assignments are quite different from tests. Incredibly boring lectures. The textbook assigned was old and boring. I ended up just self studying the Rosen Discrete math textbook. 1 out of maybe 10 lectures he will say something useful.

His notes were disorganized and made no sense. Avoid this professor at all times. I got advice from others that he was a prof to avoid, and I thought if I worked hard in this course, I could still do it. Oh i was so wrong. It doesn't matter how much effort you put into this course. His exams and assignments had no resemblance to notes whatsoever.

His lectures are monotone, the notes he writes are useless after looking back on them a few days later. His choice in examples used in lectures are often complicated, and he would often scratch out things after second guessing himself (he changed his solution 6 times once in class). MACM is already a difficult subject, even tougher with him.

worst teacher ever. got good marks in assignment and close to average in midterm. but failed final exam but few marks which gave me D. However, the class is curved. If i calculate my marks, I should get at least C- but this guy gave me D(after curved). Does not revealed how he curved it. He cannot even teach and youtube will save you.

All the lecture notes were hand written and only formula without any explanation. Only thing he did in class was just reading that hand written notes that he wrote previously without any explanation when to use this formula. All the exam questions were not even close to the textbook practice question. Maybe cause i am CMNS major

Class is established to be curved from day one. Lectures cover both theory and code, but you need to investigate the code base on your own to get a high mark. Tom listened to the suggestions, so the project work load has been reduced and you can also work in pairs. It's hard to do compilers justice in the span of 3 months, so he did a great job.

Lots of work for the compiler project but mostly due to trying to understand the code and trial and error. Apparently project has been made easier than prev years. Prof spent lectures at start of the term going over how to do certain parts of the project which was helpful to get started. Pretty accessible outside class for help. Lectures were ok.

(Fall 2023) Tbh: avoid. Dry lectures with no recorded lectures for review, and no pre-posted slides. You'll need to rely on the textbook and the scanned lect. notes. Def read ahead in the textbook before lectures and hope you get a good TA to help explain things. Midterm and final averages were both low 50s, so take that as you will. Good luck!

Course is no joke - 2 courses worth of workload packed into 1. And it's already been watered down by a LOT. Project is not dreadfully difficult as myth suggests - just a lot of trial and error + having to understand the codebase = very time consuming. Prof is knowledgeable and always available. Lectures are meh. You will come out a better dev.

MACM ON STEROIDS FOR NO REASON Reasons why you should avoid this prof at all costs: 1. Be ready to self teach. f* his lectures of hand written notes and stick to the textbook 2. You will be confused. The assignments introduce concepts that are taught later down the road and some are not based on the lectures 3. The curve will be your only hope GLHF

This man is built like Santa Claus would not be surprise if his side hustle was the Santa in the mall but the difference from him and Santa is that Shermer is a walking L. Buddy is teaching half of MACM 201 and the homework has things we did not cover. The only gift he gives is he curves the marks. Please avoid this robot at all cost.
Half the time I had no clue what his ramblings where even about. Barely gave coherent examples in the lectures. Get ready to read like you never read before if you're taking macm101 with Shermy

You'll probably end up self-learning all the materials; attending his lectures won't help. His notes don't make much sense when you read it on your own, and since his lectures aren't recorded, then you're left puzzled when reading them. Assignments are vague, even the TAs couldn't do some questions; they would provide solutions copied from online.

He did 2 hour lectures and 1 hour problem sessions. Lectures were hard to follow as they were very dense and mathematical. I often found myself not fully understanding one point then being lost for the rest of the lecture. Definitely a class where you rely heavily on the textbook. Posts lecture slides but no recordings or problem solutions.

Fairly low workload, but very challenging with Shermer. He doesn't teach anything so your best bet is to read all the textbook sections he says the exams will be on and do as many textbook problems as you can. Everyone does pretty poorly objectively, but the course is graded on a curve so you pretty much just have to outperform your peers.

Brutal course with horrible prof, impossible to tell what exam questions will be like because Shermer provides no practice exams. Exams are are extremely difficult and just understanding algorithms is not enough. Workload is fair with only 4 assignments, but content is very challenging. Course is 100% self-study as lectures are useless.

He just read his PowerPoint in all the lectures. This course will definitely be useless in my future life. It is extremely bad. This is the worst class I have ever taken. I really want to give him a 0 rate. I WILL NEVER TAKE ANY COURSES TAUGHT BY THIS PROF.

This prof is very unorganized, there is miscommunication between the prof and the TA's, we didn't get our first assignment until a month and a half into the semester. Shermer just reads off the slides and barely shows any coding. Avoid this prof if you actually want to learn something in this course.

He tried to make lectures online, but he speaks a normal talking speed if you speed up the video to x2. That is if you watch the lectures at all. They do NOT explain any of the material, they are an introduction to SOME important subjects, and don't clarify anything. His office hours are him saying re-read the chapters.
We're a month into the semester and so far he has been quite bad at conveying the material in my opinion and also the way he speaks puts you to sleep. His notes are also quite bad.

Take this class if it's your only class, his lectures leave you brittle like glass. Enters the room with a coke, always has his notes hidden behind a cloak. Learn to write fast, or you'll forget everything like it's in the past. Focus on his assignments, work til you need a back alignment. I passed but I still dislike his teaching like Comcast.

Tom is a strong believer that the best way to learn how something works is by implementing it. In this course, we started with a setPixel() method, and ended it with an implementation of a 3D model renderer (subset of .obj files) and a graphics specification language for composing scenes. It was a lot of work, but was well worth it.

Start project early - it's a lot of work. Project is built upon throughout the semester and applies what you learned in class. Lectures can get confusing but the application of the concepts to the project helps. Don't be afraid to ask Tom for help when you get stuck, he's really kind. Final project is pretty cool. Not OpenGL based.
Try to avoid his class, assignments and exams were hard and he curves down the marks!!!! The most boring class and worst prof I have ever had.

You won't learn much theories about compilers, but will totally strengthen your programming. So, if you don't "die" for programming, never think about this course. The worst thing is that he doesn't clarify how to do his assignments, you have to ask him by email or in office hours.

Made a difficult course even harder. He would go through long examples but would sometimes be unable to finish it or explain a theorem(from another chapter entirely!) needed to finish it. Notes are unclear and disorganized, sit at the front to see the notes and to hear him. Last day of class he covered two remaining chapters, badly.

I did not have a problem with his teaching. It was straightforward and he taught you what you needed to know for the assignments and exams. Unfortunately, his TA for the semester was totally useless. The assignments were hefty though, so for this class, make sure you start right away, because you will be doing coding all-nighters anyways.

Watch the videos from this guy called Jim Kurose, he has an entire playlist that is better than going to any lecture. No need to go to class if you want to learn. Although the prof seemed nice, the lectures just aren't good.
worst professor ever, just wants you to memorize stuff and then proceeds to not support the tutorial times that he set up

Entire class is practically just plagiarized from Jim Kurose, including all the slides. Homework assignments are all copy/pasted from other schools or textbooks. Exams are the same and contain content that has not been covered in class.

One of the laziest professors at SFU. Course materials patched from different schools, labs are blindly copy and pasting, no reasoning given for docked marks on exams/assignments, TAs ghost when asking for assignment feedback, exams contain material that were barely or not covered at all. Don't recommend.

Laziest prof I've ever encountered. His lectures are useless, he doesn't respond to emails, and his exams are so unrealistic given what he "covers" in class (this is true even if you do the readings).

What a brutal experience. Bob Gill is a great example of someone who puts no effort in. Don't take this class with him. I fully expect the final to be full of questions that were never taught just like the midterm. Sad.

No effort put into anything. Midterms cover topics not mentioned in slides or book. His lectures are not worth attending, and he makes passive aggressive comments about people not attending. Labs are "type the thing into terminal, report what you see". Assignments are just basic math, except with a networking theme, not actual networking problems.

Another student who's fair review was removed. To reiterate: he is the worst kind of professor. There is next to no effort put into student's learning and many midterms topics are never taught. Look at past midterms to do well, idk about the final. Don't go to class unless you are very passionate about the material.

It seems like the professor is reviewing all of them because my last review got removed. Shame. TL;DR: don't take this class if you actually want to learn, homework is copied from other schools, exams cover topics that haven't been taught, lectures go off topic a lot. At least he's funny though.

Well it seems that the prof or someone else is reporting all negative ratings on the site. All negative ratings were 'reviewed'. My rating was removed for no reason. Seems like RMP hurts his ego or something. He should take that effort and put it towards being a better prof.

I appreciate his funny storytelling, but he has a hard time lecturing about relevant course material, making it tough to actually learn. His exams and assignments are confusing and difficult. I would wait for a better prof if you want to take the course.
Wonderful prof. Nothing wrong with him at all!

One of few teachers who used Industry examples relating to course material. This made learning better. One can see applications in daily life to understand better. Thanks - may be see you in 471. Wireshark learning was great.

Bob is knowledgeable in his field. He often jokes about his experiences, but oftentimes forgets that talking about something isn't teaching it. He often glosses over very important concepts and goes on side rambles, making it hard to know what we actually need to understand. Self learning (Jim Kurose YT videos) are more helpful than Bob's lectures.
Professor Gill is truly one of the nicest professors at SFU, he genuinely cares about his students well being. His practical examples make his classes very interesting and easy to follow.

While attendance is not mandatory but If you want to learn applications to industry yo should attend his lectures. Booking knowledge is not enough. Nice Video from AlcaTel Lucent - Dr. Gill. Made additional time to teach - Thank you. some students like me.

He related Industry application to this Math Heavy course and makes sense. My car radio is a demodulator - Hmmmm I see and now I know why some equipment costs more and sounds and performs better. Looking forward to working with you. Math is mind-numbing but seeing the output is brilliant.
Nice prof and great project - didn't know about AppDesigb=ner - Nice kit for project. I learnt practical things. Thank you Prof

Puts in no effort for this class and doesn't give a damn. Homework and exams are all copy pasted from elsewhere. Doesn't even update date in PDF headers. All exams contains questions on subjects that were not taught. What is taught is half useless due to weird focus on construction and city bylaws which are irrelevant for SFU engineering.
Bob is a great prof for this course. One of the easiest courses I've taken at SFU

Bob is a chill prof. It's ensc204, you draw a couple of things and you get an easy pass. idk how you fail the class but according to Bob a student did that twice lol. Legit the only way to fail is if you ghost the class or can't draw straight lines for your life.
Good teacher

I was familiar with Project Management but Bob's lectures really prepared me for a layout plan for a startup company. Benefit-Cost Ratio, Critical path and Economic Analysis was of your company is a great skill to have. Bob Brough Guest Speakers to talk about real-life projects and their management was excellent.

Bob consistently fails to meet even the basic requirements of teaching a course. Every week he was late posting the assignment. The marking for the assignments was opaque and unclear. Oftentimes questions were so poorly worded it was impossible to interpret what was being asked, and when emailed for clarification he says he "doesn't help students".

He managed to make an incredibly boring class about the business and ethics of engineering an actual enjoyable course. His vast knowledge from working at multiple big names in the industry and his very approachable and upbeat attitude makes him a fantastic prof. His experience also allows him to give personal examples; which is rare in the program.
Bob Gill is a very nice professor, though his lectures aren't as good as they could be. He's very friendly and is willing to help his students.

Most of his lecture didnt add much to the lecture notes if you took time to look through them yourself but it was his spirit and passion for what he teaches that kept me coming to his lectures! A genuinely nice and super easygoing person that obviously had a ton of experience/knowledge about the topic he was teaching. Love him!!

He's a nice guy but... Feels like about 50% of lecture is wasted on tangents and telling the same anecdotes repeatedly. PowerPoint jockey, glacial pace. Doesn't take time to understand student questions - just answers what he thinks is being asked. Misleading analogies. Avoid if you want to learn, but not the worst if you just need the credit.

He is a very nice and friendly guy. He knows his stuff, but his teaching is confusing and all over the place. He is unorganized and you will do well if you just read the textbook. His tests and assignments are not too hard.
Nice and knowledgeable guy but terrible at teaching. Seriously not recommended, useless class. Content not overly difficult, so you are better off with reading the book on your own.

This guy's really nice, but makes mistakes everywhere - can be a bit difficult to follow. Has a tendency to go off on long tangents and then run out of class-time. His class is fun, though, especially if it's just a few students. His tests usually let you pick 4 or 5 questions out of 7 or 8, which makes them much easier.

Really nice guy, but he's a terrible professor. I was hoping for a class on Programming Languages, given the name of the course and all, but instead got a class on abstract math. I'm used to that at this point, but the fact that he was too confused to continue his lectures most of the time made it pretty hard to take. Sign up with a different prof.
Boring, unclear, makes plenty of mistakes in his lecture notes, messy lecture notes. AVOID!!!!!!!

He is the worst of the worst! Avoid him by all means. His lectures make you more confused. His writing is messy and he often makes mistakes. It's better to read the textbook on your own. He is very unorganized and unclear. There's no homework for 1 month and 2 homework in 6 days. He should be fired.
His lectures are useless
one of the weirdest teacher i've known, maybe he's smart but he doesnt know how to teach...His lectures are all over the place, I dont konw what he's talking about....bottom line, sucks
Worse professor I've ever had in my entire life. He definitely knows his stuffs, but does not know how to teach...at all.
Generally a good guy, but makes many many mistakes when he teaches
Super easy tests.goes on&on&off topic.quote: urhhh often hangs like computers
He bailed on the class after spending 2 weeks talking about admin.
Be prepared to master Haskell. Not the hottest skill in the world.
This guy spent the first 3 classes talking about his e-mail policies.
Easy Professor.
No Comments

so I thought the class wasn't bad till I did 125 and come to find thank god we only learned turtle cause that was super useful. You essentially dont learn how to code and it will make 125 terrible but he seems good at the time
Unless the final, it was pretty easy. He explains everything clearly.

Teacher doesnt really teach well to be honest. However he is very nice and kind but his teaching style and voice in lectures is difficult to understand. Doing practice exams and working hard is your best bet for this class.
Great prof. Very helpful outside of classes. Gives good feedback. For Cmpt 120 but it doesnt let me choose it

CMPT376W was quite easy with Fred. He had some interesting stories from time to time and was very nice about mixups with submissions. If you are going to take this class you should take it with him. It will be pretty easy and straight forward but if you're in CS you probably don't like writing courses anyways.

The professor is nice, but the grade you get depends on the TA. If you have Brian Booth, drop the course. He gives ridiculous reasons for taking points off, and marks some people harsher than others. Absolutely ridiculous marker! He's just a grad student, but it's harder to make him listen to your side than a full-time professor.
Fred's got a great sense of humour. Very easy marker. I'm a fob and still pulled an A. If English is your first language there's no reason why you cannot get an A+.
Fred is a great professor for this course. He was very clear and made this writing course somewhat enjoyable. Highly recommend for this course.
One of the best prof I have had from all the CMPT course I took in SFU. He explained the concepts well and was always prepared before class. Marking scheme is nice and easy.
Distance ed - Never saw him ever. TA's had bad attitudes. Didn't respond to WEB CT and didn't even give us their SFU emails. Class is not for anyone who doesn't have programming background.

nice prof :) always happy and has interest in what hes teaching. email him questions, he will answer them and try to help you as much as possible. TAs don't help, they gave a bad attitude. His lectures are from the courseware, he makes questions and does his own examples showing you step by step so you can do the hw. good prof! :)highly recommend h

Distance version. Course materials are fairly well written. Assignments are exceedingly easy, especially if you've taken another programming course and have done web design. TAs don't check WebCT, but are fairly quick at responding to email. The suggested pacing of the course is a bit off, as the bulk of the reading and work are in the final weeks.

Well... he is a good prof I guess, I took the class for a grade booster and the only problem with his teaching that I had is that he constantly threw in his biasisms into the lecture, everything from the US to Windows.

He was a good prof- very interested in the course.The lectures were well prepared but he moved on with material very quickly-it was somehow hard to keep up-midterm was very easy-but the final-a wake up call.
Very enthusiastic, very interested in the topic, gives very good lectures. Assignments were reasonable and well-related. Readily available for help.
Very enthusiastic and knowledgeable in the material. The assignments and project were manageable and closely related to the class material. He uses powerpoint.

He doesn't really know how to verbally teach materials in an organized way though his PP slices r well formed. Don't feel learning much from lec but reading the study guide. The exams were too theory focused as it's anyways a CMPT science course(Not enough programming Qs) Don't like having a big assig. due on the very last day of the semester!
talk too fast

horrible. this class was all over the place. the assignments were based on 1-2 examples that didn't give you enough information. will not return emails. the text books were poorly written. all around horrible.

What I saw in his lecture, this teacher really don't know how to use computer. However, he is teaching computer course... One more thing, if you not really master on the subject which u taking.. or you never take this subject before.. you better don't take this teacher, his hw, assignment, mid-term and exam is extremely hard!
Always comes in with a smile. High enthusiasm for teaching. However, the course is joke - good for the computer illiterates.
What a horrible professor. Lack of interest and pure neglect for the teaching aspect of computational linguistics.
